summary: Metaphysical English poet, Dean of St. Paul's. Known for the Holy Sonnets (including "Death, be not proud" and "Batter my heart"), "A Valediction: Forbidding Mourning" (compass conceit), "The Flea," "The Canonization," and Devotions upon Emergent Occasions ("no man is an island").

works.name: A Valediction: Forbidding Mourning
works.description: Poem written for his wife Anne More, using the famous compass conceit.

clues.clue: Compares parting lovers to "stiff twin compasses" — "Thy firmness makes my circle just"
examples: compares two souls to "swift twin compasses"
"Thy firmness makes my circle just"

clues.clue: Opens "As virtuous men pass mildly away"
examples: opens 'as virtuous men pass mildly away'

clues.clue: Criticizes "dull sublunary lovers' love" that "cannot admit of absence"
examples: "dull sublunary lovers' love"

clues.clue: Describes parting as "not a breach, but an expansion / Like gold to airy thinness beat"
examples: "gold to airy thinness beat"

works.name: Holy Sonnets
works.description: Collection of 19 religious sonnets including "Death, be not proud" and "Batter my heart, three-person'd God."

clues.clue: "Death, be not proud" — Death is "slave to fate, chance, kings, and desperate men"
examples: "slave to Fate, Chance, kings, and desperate men"

clues.clue: "Batter my heart" — asks God to "break, blow, burn and make me new"; "nor ever chaste, except you ravish me"
examples: "break, blow, burn, and make me new"

clues.clue: "Batter my heart" — reason as weak "viceroy"; speaker is like "an usurp'd town"
examples: compares "reason" to a "viceroy"

clues.clue: "I am a little world made cunningly" — asks God to "pour new seas in mine eyes"
examples: "pour new seas in mine eyes"

clues.clue: Sonnet about wife Anne's death: "Since she whom I lov'd hath paid her last debt"
examples: "Since she whom I lov'd hath paid her last debt"

works.name: The Canonization
works.description: Poem defending the speaker's love, opening "For God's sake hold your tongue, and let me love."

clues.clue: Opens "For God's sake hold your tongue, and let me love"
examples: "For God's sake hold your tongue, and let me love"

clues.clue: "A well-wrought urn becomes / The greatest ashes" — inspired Cleanth Brooks's title
examples: "a well-wrought urn becomes / The greatest ashes"

works.name: The Flea
works.description: Poem arguing for physical intimacy using an insect conceit.

clues.clue: A creature that "sucked me first, and now sucks thee" — blood mingling
examples: "sucked me first, and now sucks thee"

clues.clue: Asks if the addressee has "purpled thy nail, in blood of innocence"
examples: "purpled thy nail, in blood of innocence"

works.name: To His Mistress Going to Bed
works.description: Erotic elegy comparing the lover's body to exploring America.

clues.clue: Calls the lover's body "my America! my new-found-land"
examples: "my America! my new-found-land"

clues.clue: Tells lover to "license my roving hands" and "unpin that spangled breast-plate"
examples: "license my roving hands"

works.name: Songs and Sonnets
works.description: Collection including "Go and catch a falling star," "The Good-Morrow," and "The Sun Rising."

clues.clue: "Go and catch a falling star" — impossible tasks: "Get with child a mandrake root"
examples: "Get with child a mandrake root"

clues.clue: "The Good-Morrow" — "I wonder, by my troth, what thou and I did, till we loved?"
examples: "I wonder, by my troth, what thou and I did, till we loved?"

clues.clue: "The Sun Rising" — calls the sun a "busy old fool" and "saucy pedantic wretch"
examples: "busy old fool" and a "saucy pedantic wretch"

works.name: Devotions upon Emergent Occasions
works.description: Prose meditations written during illness, containing Meditation XVII.

clues.clue: "No man is an island" and "never send to know for whom the bell tolls"
examples: "no man is an island"

clues.clue: Provided the title for Hemingway's For Whom the Bell Tolls
examples: provided the title of Hemingway's For Whom the Bell Tolls

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Metaphysical poet, Dean of St. Paul's, secretly married Anne More.

clues.clue: Secretly married Anne More
examples: John Donne secretly married a woman with the surname More

clues.clue: Dean of St. Paul's; delivered "Death's Duel" as his final sermon while fatally ill
examples: he had "preached his own funeral sermon"

clues.clue: Subject of Joseph Brodsky elegy describing him as having "sunk in sleep"
examples: this author has "sunk in sleep"

clues.clue: T.S. Eliot praised his "unified sensibility" in "The Metaphysical Poets"
examples: Eliot's "The Metaphysical Poets" praises this author's "unified sensibility"

clues.clue: Izaak Walton wrote his biography
examples: Izaak Walton describes how he delivered "Death's Duel"

comprehensive_summary:
John Donne is a Metaphysical English poet and Dean of St. Paul's Cathedral. His most frequently clued work is "A Valediction: Forbidding Mourning," written for his wife Anne More, which compares parting lovers to "stiff twin compasses" with the line "Thy firmness makes my circle just." The poem opens "As virtuous men pass mildly away" and criticizes "dull sublunary lovers' love."

His Holy Sonnets are heavily clued. "Death, be not proud" declares Death is "slave to fate, chance, kings, and desperate men." "Batter my heart, three-person'd God" asks God to "break, blow, burn and make me new" and ends "nor ever chaste, except you ravish me," with Reason described as a weak "viceroy." "I am a little world made cunningly" asks God to "pour new seas in mine eyes."

"The Canonization" opens "For God's sake hold your tongue, and let me love" and contains "a well-wrought urn becomes / The greatest ashes," inspiring Cleanth Brooks's book title. "The Flea" uses a creature that "sucked me first, and now sucks thee." "To His Mistress Going to Bed" calls the lover's body "my America! my new-found-land."

From Songs and Sonnets, "Go and catch a falling star" lists impossible tasks. "The Good-Morrow" opens "I wonder, by my troth, what thou and I did, till we loved?" Devotions upon Emergent Occasions contains "no man is an island" and "for whom the bell tolls," providing Hemingway's title. Donne secretly married Anne More and delivered "Death's Duel" as his final sermon. Joseph Brodsky wrote an elegy describing Donne as having "sunk in sleep."
